event: Aboriginal peoples arrive on the Australian continent (Sahul) via maritime crossings from Southeast Asia, beginning one of the world's oldest continuous human cultures.
Humans occupy sites across the continent, including Lake Mungo in New South Wales, where ritual burials and cremations indicate complex spiritual practices.
Aboriginal communities produce rock art in regions such as Arnhem Land and the Kimberley, among the oldest artistic traditions on Earth.
During the Last Glacial Maximum, lower sea levels keep Tasmania and New Guinea connected to mainland Australia.
Rising seas at the end of the Ice Age sever the land bridges, isolating Tasmanian Aboriginal peoples and flooding the Bass Strait and Torres Strait.
The dingo is introduced to mainland Australia, likely via seafarers from Southeast Asia, reshaping ecosystems and Aboriginal hunting.
Dutch navigator Willem Janszoon makes the first documented European landfall on the Australian coast at Cape York Peninsula aboard the Duyfken.
Dutch captain Dirk Hartog lands on the Western Australian coast and leaves an inscribed pewter plate, the oldest known European artefact in Australia.
Abel Tasman charts Van Diemen's Land (Tasmania) and New Zealand for the Dutch East India Company.
English buccaneer William Dampier becomes the first Englishman to set foot on Australia, on the northwest coast.
Lieutenant James Cook charts the eastern coast aboard HMS Endeavour and claims the territory for Britain as New South Wales at Possession Island.
The First Fleet under Governor Arthur Phillip establishes a British penal colony at Sydney Cove on 26 January, marking the start of European settlement.
British settlement begins in Van Diemen's Land (Tasmania), expanding the convict system southward.
Blaxland, Lawson, and Wentworth cross the Blue Mountains, opening the interior of New South Wales to pastoral expansion.
The Brisbane River penal settlement is founded, leading to the colony of Queensland.
Britain establishes the Swan River Colony, the beginning of permanent settlement in Western Australia.
John Batman's settlement on the Yarra River leads to the founding of Melbourne.
The Colony of South Australia is founded as a planned free settlement rather than a penal colony.
Gold is discovered in New South Wales and Victoria, triggering gold rushes that transform the economy and dramatically increase immigration.
Gold miners at Ballarat rebel against colonial authorities in the Eureka Stockade, a landmark moment for Australian democracy.
Several colonies adopt the secret ballot and responsible self-government, pioneering reforms later copied worldwide.
The transportation of British convicts to Australia formally ends with the last convict ship arriving in Western Australia.
Bushranger Ned Kelly is captured and hanged in Melbourne, becoming an enduring figure of Australian folklore.
The six British colonies federate to form the Commonwealth of Australia on 1 January, with Edmund Barton as the first prime minister.
Australia grants most women the right to vote and stand for federal parliament, among the earliest nations to do so.
Canberra is selected as the site for the new national capital.
Australian and New Zealand troops land at Gallipoli, a costly WWI campaign that becomes central to the Anzac national legend.
Federal Parliament is opened in the new capital, Canberra, moving the seat of government from Melbourne.
The Sydney Harbour Bridge opens during the Great Depression as a symbol of national resilience.
Australia enters World War II alongside Britain after the German invasion of Poland.
Japanese forces bomb Darwin and the Australian and Allied forces halt the Japanese advance along the Kokoda Track in New Guinea.
Australia becomes a founding member of the United Nations and emerges from WWII more closely aligned with the United States.
The Nationality and Citizenship Act creates Australian citizenship as distinct from British subject status.
Australia signs the ANZUS security treaty with the United States and New Zealand.
Britain begins atomic weapons testing on Australian soil at the Montebello Islands and later Maralinga, affecting Aboriginal communities.
Melbourne hosts the Summer Olympic Games, the first held in the Southern Hemisphere.
A landmark referendum allows Aboriginal people to be counted in the census and the Commonwealth to make laws for them, passing with over 90 percent support.
Aboriginal activists establish the Tent Embassy in Canberra, and the Whitlam government dismantles the White Australia immigration policy.
The Sydney Opera House is opened by Queen Elizabeth II after years of construction.
Governor-General John Kerr dismisses Prime Minister Gough Whitlam in an unprecedented constitutional crisis, and Papua New Guinea gains independence from Australia.
The Australia Act severs remaining legislative and judicial ties with the United Kingdom, completing constitutional independence.
The High Court's Mabo decision recognises native title, overturning the legal doctrine of terra nullius.
Australians vote in a referendum to retain the monarchy, rejecting a proposal to become a republic.
Sydney hosts the Summer Olympic Games, showcasing Australia on the world stage.
Prime Minister Kevin Rudd delivers a formal national apology to the Stolen Generations of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples.
The Black Saturday bushfires in Victoria kill 173 people, among the deadliest in Australian history.
The catastrophic Black Summer bushfires burn vast areas, kill billions of animals, and intensify debate over climate change.
Australia confronts the COVID-19 pandemic with strict border closures and prolonged lockdowns, notably in Victoria.
Labor returns to power under Anthony Albanese, ending nearly a decade of Coalition government.
Australians reject the Indigenous Voice to Parliament referendum, declining to enshrine an Aboriginal advisory body in the constitution.
